lists.title: Hausa A1 Grammar
lists.description: Foundations: pronouns, ne/ce, the aspect system (completive, continuous, future), existence, negation, the genitive linker.

lists.title: Hausa A2 Grammar
lists.description: Plurals, object and possessive pronouns, commands, habituals.

lists.title: Hausa B1 Grammar
lists.description: Subjunctive, relative clauses, the -ke set, conditionals.

lists.title: Hausa B2 Grammar
lists.description: Impersonal an/ana and comparison with fi.

lists.title: Hausa C2 Grammar
lists.description: Mastery: karin magana, greeting protocol, and the particles that carry tone of voice.

points.title: Independent pronouns (ni, kai, ke…)
points.explanation: The free pronouns are ni (I), kai (you, m), ke (you, f), shi (he), ita (she), mu (we), ku (you all), su (they). Hausa marks gender in the second and third person singular — kai to a man, ke to a woman.

points.title: Identity with ne / ce
points.function: Say what someone or something is
points.explanation: Identity sentences end in ne (masculine/plural) or ce (feminine): Ni malami ne (I am a teacher), Ita malama ce (She is a teacher), Su ɗalibai ne (They are students). The particle agrees with the SUBJECT noun's gender.
points.culture_note: Hausa has two genders, masculine and feminine — most feminines end in -a, and ne/ce is your first meeting with that system.

drills.hint: which particle? check gender/number of the noun
which particle? check gender/number of the noun
which particle? check gender/number of the noun
which particle? check gender/number of the noun

points.title: Completive aspect (na, ka, ya, ta…)
points.explanation: Hausa conjugates with aspect pronouns before the verb. Completive (did/has done): na (I), ka/kin (you m/f), ya (he), ta (she), mun (we), kun (you all), sun (they): Na ci abinci (I ate food), Sun tafi (They left).

points.title: Continuous aspect (ina, kana, yana…)
points.explanation: The continuous uses -na pronouns plus the verbal noun: Ina cin abinci (I am eating), Yana aiki (He is working), Muna zuwa (We are coming). Ina also opens 'where' questions: Ina kake? (Where are you?).

points.title: Future with zan, za ka…
points.explanation: The future combines za with the person: zan (I will), za ka/za ki, zai (he), za ta, za mu, za ku, za su: Zan tafi gida (I will go home), Za ta dawo gobe (She will return tomorrow).

drills.translation: We will go to the market.
They will return in the evening.

points.title: Existence: akwai / babu
points.function: Say what there is and isn't
points.explanation: Akwai asserts existence, babu denies it: Akwai ruwa (There is water), Babu kuɗi (There is no money). With 'have' meaning: Akwai littafi a wurina (I have a book — lit. there's a book at my place); everyday 'have' also uses da: Ina da mota (I have a car).

drills.translation: There are many people at the market.
There is no food at home.
Is there water in the well?

points.title: Negation ba … ba
points.explanation: Most negation wraps the clause in ba … ba: Ban ci ba (I didn't eat — ba+na fuses to ban), Ba ta zo ba (She didn't come). The continuous negates with bā (single): Ba na zuwa (I am not coming/going).

drills.hint: the closing half of the frame
the closing half of the frame
the opening half of the frame
the closing half of the frame

points.title: The genitive linker -n / -r
points.explanation: Possession links nouns with -n (masculine/plural) or -r (feminine): littafin Audu (Audu's book, littafi+n), motar Audu (Audu's car, mota+r), gidan malami (the teacher's house). The linker matches the FIRST noun's gender.

points.title: Questions (me, wa, ina)
points.explanation: Me (what), wa (who), ina (where), yaushe (when), nawa (how much): Me ka ci? (What did you eat?), Wa ya zo? (Who came?), Ina kake? (Where are you?). Yes/no questions add ko…? or just rising intonation: Ka ci abinci? / Ko ka ci?

points.title: Common plurals
points.explanation: Hausa plurals change the word's shape, often with -oCi or internal -a-: mota → motoci (cars), littafi → littattafai (books), gida → gidaje (houses), yaro → yara (children). Each noun's plural is learned with it — patterns help but don't fully predict.

drills.translation: The women are at the market.
The farmers went to the farm.

points.title: Object pronouns
points.explanation: After the verb: ni (me), ka/ki (you m/f), shi (him), ta (her), mu (us), ku (you all), su (them): Na gan shi (I saw him), Sun ji mu (They heard us). Many verbs shorten before an object: gani → gan (see).

drills.translation: I saw him at the market.

points.title: Possessive pronouns (-na, -nka…)
points.explanation: Possessives attach through the linker: littafina (my book), littafinka/ki (your, m/f owner), littafinsa (his), littafinta (her); feminine nouns use -r-: motata (my car), motarsa (his car). Gender of the THING picks -n-/-r-; the ending shows the owner.

points.title: Imperative and don't (kada)
points.explanation: The bare verb commands: Zo! (Come!), Tafi! (Go!), Ci abinci! (Eat!). The negative command uses kada + subjunctive pronoun: Kada ka tafi! (Don't go!), Kada ku manta! (Don't (you all) forget!).

drills.hint: the subjunctive pronoun after the prohibitive

points.title: Habitual (nakan, yakan…)
points.explanation: The habitual pronoun set adds -kan: nakan (I usually), kakan, yakan, takan, mukan, kukan, sukan: Nakan sha shayi da safe (I usually drink tea in the morning), Yakan zo nan (He usually comes here).

drills.translation: I usually drink tea in the morning.
He usually comes here every day.
She usually cooks in the evening.
Do you (masculine) usually drink a lot of water?

points.title: Subjunctive (ka tafi, mu je…)
points.explanation: The plain (subjunctive) pronoun set — in, ka/ki, ya, ta, mu, ku, su — follows wanting and purpose: Ina so ka zo (I want you to come), Bari mu je (Let's go), Ya kamata ka yi aiki (You should work).

drills.translation: I want them to go now.

points.title: Relative clauses (wanda, wadda, waɗanda)
points.explanation: Wanda (m), wadda (f), waɗanda (pl) introduce relatives: mutumin da ya zo — or with the full pronoun: wanda ya zo (the one who came), motar da na saya (the car that I bought). In relatives the continuous switches to -ke: wanda yake aiki (who is working).

drills.translation: The man who came is a teacher.
drills.hint: the short relative after a noun

drills.translation: The one who came is a teacher.
The one (feminine) who came is a teacher.
The girl who came is here.
drills.hint: the short relative after a noun

drills.translation: The people who left are farmers.
drills.hint: the short relative after a noun

points.title: Relative continuous -ke
points.explanation: In relatives, questions with fronting, and focus, continuous -na becomes -ke: Ina kake zaune? (Where do you live?), mace take aiki? no — abin da nake yi (the thing I am doing), wanda yake magana (the one who is speaking).

drills.sentence: Abin da {{answer}} yi yana da kyau.
drills.translation: The thing I am doing is good.
Where we live is far away.
What they are seeking is money.

points.title: Conditionals with idan / in
points.explanation: Idan (or short in) opens real conditions: Idan ka zo, za mu ci abinci (If you come, we will eat), In ta gani, za ta gaya mana (If she sees, she'll tell us). Unreal 'if only' uses dā …: Dā na sani (Had I known).

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ka zo, za mu ci abinci.
drills.translation: If you come, we will eat.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ruwa ya sauka, ba za mu fita ba.
drills.translation: If it rains, we won't go out.

drills.sentence: Idan ka {{answer}}, za ka ci jarrabawa.
drills.translation: If you study, you will pass the exam.

drills.sentence: Da na {{answer}}, da na gaya maka.
drills.translation: Had I known, I would have told you.

points.title: Impersonal an / ana (passive sense)
points.function: Say what was/is done without naming who
points.explanation: The impersonal pronoun a- makes passive-like sentences: An gina gida (A house was built — 'one built'), Ana maganin sa? no — Ana kiran ka (You are being called), Za a biya ku (You will be paid).

drills.translation: A new house has been built.
You are being waited for at the office.

points.title: Comparison with fi
points.explanation: The verb fi (exceed) does comparison: Ya fi ni tsayi (He is taller than me — lit. exceeds me in height), Wannan ya fi kyau (This is better), Ta fi su duka hankali (She is the smartest of them all).

drills.translation: This one is better (exceeds in goodness).
She is more sensible than me.
A lion is stronger than a dog.
This one is more expensive than that one.

points.title: Reported speech with cewa
points.explanation: Cewa ('saying/that') introduces reported clauses: Ya ce (cewa) zai zo (He said that he would come), Na ji cewa sun tafi (I heard that they left), Ta tabbata cewa gaskiya ne (She is sure that it is true).

drills.translation: He said that he would come.

drills.sentence: Ta ce {{answer}} ba za ta zo ba.
drills.translation: She said that she would not come.

drills.sentence: Mun ji {{answer}} an gina sabuwar makaranta.
drills.translation: We heard that a new school has been built.
He said that he would help us.

drills.sentence: Na yi imani {{answer}} za su dawo.
drills.translation: I believe that they will return.

points.title: Focus fronting with ne/ce
points.explanation: Fronting plus ne/ce focuses: Audu ne ya zo (It was AUDU who came), Kasuwa ce ta tafi? no — Jiya ne suka tafi (It was yesterday they left; completive shifts to the relative set suka). Gender/number of the focused word picks ne or ce.

drills.hint: focus particle — masculine/plural or non-feminine focus

drills.translation: It was yesterday that they left.
drills.hint: focus particle — masculine/plural or non-feminine focus

drills.translation: It was she who cooked the food.
It was at the market that I saw him.
drills.hint: focus particle — masculine/plural or non-feminine focus

drills.translation: It was Audu who wrote the letter.
drills.hint: focus particle — masculine/plural or non-feminine focus

drills.translation: It is today that we will go.
drills.hint: focus particle — masculine/plural or non-feminine focus

points.title: Demonstratives (wannan, wancan, waɗannan)
points.function: Point at this one, that one, these and those
points.explanation: Wannan (this), wancan (that, m.) / waccan (that, f.), waɗannan (these), waɗancan (those); the bare adverbs nan (here) and can (there) do places. The demonstrative comes BEFORE the noun: wannan littafi.

points.title: To have: da (ina da, ba ni da)
points.function: Say what you have and what you lack
points.explanation: Possession rides the continuous plus da: ina da mota (I have a car), yana da kuɗi (he has money). The negative uses the short pronoun set: ba ni da kuɗi (I don't have money), ba shi da lokaci.

drills.hint: the possession linker after the continuous
the possession linker after the continuous

drills.translation: I don't have money right now.

drills.hint: the possession linker after the continuous
the possession linker after the continuous
the possession linker after the continuous

points.title: Describing with mai / masu
points.function: Turn any quality into an adjective
points.explanation: Mai (owner of) + a noun describes: gida mai kyau (a house having goodness — a nice house), shayi mai zaƙi (sweet tea). The plural is masu: mutane masu arziki (rich people). It's Hausa's most productive adjective machine.

drills.sentence: Mutane {{answer}} arziki suna zaune a nan.
Manoma {{answer}} aiki tuƙuru sun sami riba.

points.title: Relative completive (suka, muka, aka)
points.explanation: After the relative da (and in focus), the completive switches form: sun zo (they came) but mutanen da SUKA zo (the people who came). Watch sun→suka, mun→muka, kun→kuka, an→aka; ya and ta keep their spelling.

drills.sentence: Mutanen da {{answer}} zo jiya sun tafi.
drills.translation: The people who came yesterday have left.

drills.sentence: Abincin da {{answer}} dafa yana da daɗi.
drills.translation: The food we cooked is delicious.
Where is the book you (pl.) bought?

drills.sentence: Gidan da {{answer}} gina yana da kyau.
drills.translation: The house that was built is beautiful.

drills.sentence: Yaron da {{answer}} ci jarrabawa yana murna.
drills.translation: The boy who passed the exam is rejoicing.
I like the song that she sang.

points.title: Numbers after the noun (biyu, goma, nawa)
points.function: Count things and ask how many
points.explanation: Numerals follow the noun: mota biyu (two cars), kwana bakwai (seven days). Nawa asks 'how many/how much' from the same slot — littattafai nawa? (how many books?), nawa ne? (how much is it?).

drills.translation: There is only one car at the house.
There are seven days in a week.
How many books do you have?

points.title: Sai — until, then, only, except
points.function: Master Hausa's most versatile little word
points.explanation: Sai covers a family of meanings: farewell (sai gobe — see you tomorrow), 'not until' (ba za mu fara ba sai ya zo), 'only when/then' (sai ka gama za ka huta), and 'except' (kowa ya tafi sai Audu). Context picks the reading.
points.culture_note: Sai an jima (until later), sai gobe, sai da safe — most Hausa goodbyes are sai-phrases.

drills.hint: the versatile until/then word — farewell use

drills.sentence: Ba za mu fara ba {{answer}} ya zo.
drills.translation: We won't start until he comes.
drills.hint: the until word after a negative

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ka gama aiki za ka huta.
drills.translation: Only when you finish work will you rest.

drills.sentence: In ba haka ba, {{answer}} wata rana.
drills.translation: If not, then some other day.

points.title: Verbal nouns in the continuous (cin, shan, zuwa)
points.function: Say what's happening right now, properly
points.explanation: The continuous wants a verbal noun: ina CIN abinci (I'm eating), yana SHAN ruwa (he's drinking), muna ZUWA gida (we're going home). Common verbs have special forms (ci→cin, sha→shan, zo→zuwa, ji→jin); most others keep the plain stem before an object.

drills.hint: to write — plain stem before an object

drills.translation: They are working on the farm.
drills.hint: work — a plain noun after the continuous

points.title: Because and in order to: don, domin, saboda
points.explanation: Don/domin + subjunctive states purpose (na zo don in gan ka — I came to see you); domin + clause and saboda + noun give reasons (saboda ruwan sama — because of the rain). Don me? asks why.

drills.translation: I came in order to see you.

drills.sentence: Ya yi murna {{answer}} ya ci jarrabawa.
drills.translation: He rejoiced because he passed the exam.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ruwan sama, ba mu fita ba.
drills.translation: Because of the rain, we didn't go out.

drills.sentence: Muna aiki tuƙuru {{answer}} mu sami kuɗi.
drills.translation: We work hard in order to earn money.
She bought a gift for her son.
Why (for what) did you do that?
drills.hint: the for word — asks why with 'me'

points.title: Before and after: kafin, bayan
points.explanation: Kafin (before) takes the subjunctive — kafin ka tafi (before you go); bayan (after) takes the completive — bayan ya gama (after he finished). Bayan haka: after that.

drills.translation: Before you go, turn off the light.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ya gama aiki, ya koma gida.
drills.translation: After he finished work, he went home.

drills.sentence: Za mu ci abinci {{answer}} mu yi barci.
drills.translation: We will eat before we sleep.
After they got up, they prayed.

drills.sentence: Ka wanke hannu {{answer}} ka ci abinci.
drills.translation: Wash your hands before you eat.

points.title: Connectors kuma, har, ma, amma
points.explanation: Kuma adds ('and also'), har stretches ('until / as far as / even'), ma tags along ('too/even'), amma turns ('but'). They're small, but Hausa narrative runs on them.

drills.sentence: Ya sayi gurasa {{answer}} ya sayi madara.
drills.translation: He bought bread and also bought milk.

drills.sentence: Ta yi aiki {{answer}} dare ya yi.
Yana so ya zo, {{answer}} ba shi da lokaci.
drills.translation: He wants to come, but he has no time.
They travelled as far as Kano.

points.title: Ideophonic intensifiers (fari fat, ja wur)
points.function: Intensify with the word each quality owns
points.explanation: Each quality has its dedicated intensifier: fari FAT (snow-white), baƙi ƘIRIN (jet-black), ja WUR (blazing red), sabo FUL (brand-new), cika FAL (full to the brim), shiru TSIT (dead silent). The pairs are fixed — swapping them sounds absurd.
points.culture_note: Ideophones are performance: said with stretched vowels and a gesture. Regional variants exist — native reviewers should confirm local forms.

points.title: Verb pairs: saya/sayar, koya/koyar, dafa/dafu
points.function: Flip a verb's direction with its grade
points.explanation: Hausa verbs come in grades: -ar (usually + da) points the action outward — saya buy → sayar da sell, koya learn → koyar da teach; -u turns it inward/resultative — dafa cook → dafu be cooked, gina build → ginu get built.

drills.hint: to buy flipped outward (+ da) = sell
to learn flipped outward (+ da) = teach
to cook flipped inward = be cooked
to build flipped inward = get built

drills.translation: I bought a new phone yesterday.

points.title: Indirect objects (mini, maka, masa)
points.function: Do things TO and FOR people in one word
points.explanation: The wa/ma- set fuses 'to/for' with a pronoun: rubuta MINI (write to me), dafa MAKA (cook for you), aika MASA (send to him), MATA (to her), MUSU (to them). It slots right after the verb, before the direct object.

drills.translation: She will cook food for you.
They built a house for her.

points.title: Ɗan-compounds (ɗan kasuwa, ʼyan sanda)
points.explanation: Ɗan (son of) + noun names belonging or profession: ɗan Nijeriya (a Nigerian), ɗan kasuwa (trader), ɗan makaranta (pupil). Feminine ʼyar, plural ʼyan — ʼyan sanda (police, 'children of the stick'). Before a size word it shrinks things: ɗan ƙaramin gida (a smallish house).

drills.translation: Audu is a trader in Kano.
The players are on the football field.

drills.hint: the diminutive use before a size word

points.title: Karin magana — proverbs
points.function: Season your speech the Hausa way
points.explanation: Karin magana (folded speech) compresses the grammar you know into fixed wisdom: Sannu ba ta hana zuwa (going slowly doesn't prevent arriving); Haƙuri maganin duniya (patience is the world's medicine). Dropping the right one at the right moment carries real authority.
points.culture_note: Karin magana literally means 'folded speech' — meaning tucked inside meaning, unfolded by the listener.

drills.translation: Going slowly does not prevent arriving.
drills.hint: arriving — the verbal noun of going/coming

drills.translation: Patience is the medicine of the world.
However far the throw, it will fall to earth.
Water doesn't turn sour for nothing.

points.title: Greetings and respect (Ranka ya daɗe)
points.function: Greet like a person of manners
points.explanation: Hausa greeting is protocol: Ranka ya daɗe (may your life be long) to emirs and elders, barka da safiya/yamma by time of day, gafara dai to announce yourself, na gode to thank, madalla to approve, maraba da zuwa to welcome.
points.culture_note: Greetings are unskippable: rushing past sannu into business marks you as graceless. The emir's greeting Ranka ya daɗe survives in offices for any big boss.

drills.translation: 'May your life be long!' he greeted the emir.
Excuse me! I would like to come in.
Thank you very much, elder sibling.

points.title: Discourse particles (fa, dai, mana, ashe)
points.function: Carry the tone writing can't show
points.explanation: Fa bounces the topic back (kai fa? — and you?), dai hedges ('as for / just'), mana urges (zo mana! — do come!), ashe marks a realization ('so it turns out!'), kuwa underlines ('indeed'). Without them you sound like a textbook.

drills.translation: And you, how is your work?
So it was YOU who did this!
As for me, I don't agree.

points.title: Ko — or, whether, any-, right?
points.function: One little word, a whole toolkit
points.explanation: Ko joins alternatives (shayi ko kofi), embeds doubt (ban sani ba ko zai zo — I don't know whether he'll come), tags questions (ko ba haka ba? — isn't it so?), and builds the any-words: kowa (everyone), komai (everything), ko'ina (everywhere), ko kaɗan (not even a little).

drills.translation: Do you want tea or coffee?
I don't know whether he will come.

drills.sentence: Aikin ya yi kyau, {{answer}} ba haka ba?
drills.translation: The work is good, isn't it?
I don't want even a little.
